meta.title: Coffee Business Vocabulary — Spaced Repetition Deck
meta.method: Leitner box spaced repetition (5 boxes: 1/2/4/7/14 day intervals). Box 1 = MCQ recognition. Box 2+ = fill-in-the-blank recall.

cards.definition: Farmers forming their own company to pool coffee and sell it together as one unit instead of as separate, weak sellers.
cards.analogy: Like a housing society, but for selling coffee instead of managing a building.
cards.why_it_matters: If BR Hills' Soliga farmers already have one, you deal with one supplier instead of fifteen individual people.
cards.cloze: An ___ is farmers forming their own mini company to pool and sell coffee together as one unit.

cards.definition: The umbrella category of farmer collectives — could be structured as an FPC, a cooperative society, or a trust.
cards.analogy: FPO is the category 'vehicle'; FPC is one specific model of vehicle.
cards.why_it_matters: When Coffee Board or VGKK say 'farmer organisation,' ask which structure it actually is — it changes how they negotiate.
cards.cloze: ___ is the umbrella term that FPC sits under — it could be a company, cooperative, or trust.

cards.definition: An older, simpler legal structure than an FPC — members pool produce and share profits under Cooperative Societies law, often with stronger state involvement.
cards.analogy: Closer to how Araku's Girijan Cooperative Society is set up.
cards.why_it_matters: The Araku model you're studying is a cooperative, not an FPC — different governance, worth understanding before trying to replicate it in Shevaroys.
cards.cloze: The Araku Girijan model is a ___, governed under Cooperative Societies law rather than Companies law.

cards.definition: A small group (often 10–20 women) who save and lend together and increasingly run livelihood activities.
cards.analogy: A trust circle that's now also becoming a business unit.
cards.why_it_matters: If you ever explore a women-led retail angle, SHGs are existing infrastructure Coffee Board is already building — not something to create from scratch.
cards.cloze: An ___ is a small savings-and-lending group of women, often now running livelihood businesses like café kiosks.

cards.definition: National Bank for Agriculture and Rural Development — funds and supports FPC formation with working-capital loans and subsidies.
cards.analogy: The government's rural-development bank.
cards.why_it_matters: If a farmer group doesn't yet have an FPC, NABARD is often why one could exist within a year or two.
cards.cloze: ___ is the government rural-development bank that funds and subsidises the formation of FPCs.

cards.definition: Official Coffee Board registration proving you're a legitimate exporter. Valid 3 years.
cards.analogy: Like a gym membership card, but for being a recognized exporter.
cards.why_it_matters: Without it you can't formally export; with it, customs and international buyers recognize you as legitimate.
cards.cloze: An ___ is the Coffee Board certificate that officially recognizes you as an exporter, valid for 3 years.

cards.definition: Required license to operate a curing works — the facility that turns raw cherry/parchment into clean, exportable green beans.
cards.analogy: Like an FSSAI license, but coffee-specific.
cards.why_it_matters: You won't run curing yourself, but it tells you the right question to ask toll roasters and curing works: 'are you licensed, and at what scale?'
cards.cloze: A ___ is required to operate a curing works that hulls, polishes, grades and sorts coffee into green beans.

cards.definition: Registration needed to run pulping equipment at scale — pulping strips skin and pulp off fresh cherry, usually within hours of picking.
cards.analogy: The very first processing step after picking.
cards.why_it_matters: If Shevaroys smallholders pulp their own cherry rather than selling wet cherry to a central point, that signals a more independent, quality-controlled farmer — a good storytelling detail.
cards.cloze: Pulping strips the skin off fresh cherry within hours of picking; running one at scale needs a ___.

cards.definition: Runs the weekly Wednesday–Thursday Bengaluru coffee auction — a public price-discovery mechanism.
cards.analogy: The 'mandi' for coffee.
cards.why_it_matters: ICTA prices are your best public benchmark for whether a farmer's asking price is fair, without seeming like you're haggling.
cards.cloze: ___ runs the weekly Bengaluru coffee auction that sets public benchmark prices like 'Plantation A' and 'Arabica cherry AB.'

cards.definition: The global body that tracks and reports coffee statistics across producing and consuming countries — source of the I-CIP price.
cards.analogy: Coffee's version of OPEC's reporting arm, but without price-setting power.
cards.why_it_matters: It only measures and reports — it doesn't set prices, unlike a cartel.
cards.cloze: The ___ is the global body that reports coffee statistics and publishes the I-CIP price, but does not set prices itself.

cards.term: Cherry → Parchment → Green Bean → Clean Coffee
cards.definition: The bean's life stages in order: whole ripe fruit (cherry) → dried/pulped with papery husk (parchment) → hulled raw bean (green) → sorted and defect-free (clean coffee).
cards.analogy: Each stage strips away more of the fruit, so price per kg rises sharply at each step.
cards.why_it_matters: When a farmer quotes a price, always ask which stage it's for — cherry is cheapest by weight since so much gets removed later.
cards.cloze: The bean's stages in order are: Cherry → ___ → Green Bean → Clean Coffee.

cards.definition: Cherries are pulped the same day, then mucilage is broken down in fermentation tanks before washing and drying — produces a cleaner, brighter cup.
cards.analogy: This is Galaxy Frog's profile.
cards.why_it_matters: This is the benchmark 'clean' process against which honey and natural coffees are judged.
cards.cloze: The ___ process pulps cherry the same day, ferments off the mucilage, then washes and dries — giving a bright, clean cup.

cards.definition: Whole cherries are dried in the sun with fruit still intact, then hulled afterward — fruit sugars ferment into the bean, giving heavier body and fruitier flavours.
cards.analogy: The opposite extreme from washed processing.
cards.why_it_matters: Understanding this vs. washed helps you describe why a Seasonal-tier lot tastes the way it does.
cards.cloze: In ___ processing, whole cherries dry in the sun with the fruit intact, producing heavier body and wilder flavours.

cards.definition: A middle path: skin is removed but some or all mucilage is left on during drying. Named white/yellow/red/black honey by how much mucilage remains.
cards.analogy: A dial between fully washed and fully natural.
cards.why_it_matters: This is the 'monitored natural/honey' approach flagged for the Seasonal tier's first season.
cards.cloze: In ___ processing, skin is removed but mucilage is left on during drying — named white/yellow/red/black by how much remains.

cards.term: AB, PB, C, Bulk — Bean Grades
cards.definition: Grading by screen (bean) size after hulling: PB (peaberry, smaller/rounder, premium), AB (standard benchmark grade), C (smaller/more defects), Bulk (ungraded, lowest tier).
cards.analogy: Like sieving beans into size categories, from prized to bottom-tier.
cards.why_it_matters: 'AB' in a Coffee Board price table is the reference-quality grade — a good anchor for what to expect to pay at Shevaroys or BR Hills.
cards.cloze: ___ is the standard, largest-screen-size grade — the benchmark quoted at auction (e.g. 'Arabica cherry AB').

cards.definition: Counts physical defects per sample: Category 1 = serious (black, sour, insect-damaged beans); Category 2 = minor (broken, husk fragments, immature).
cards.analogy: A checkable score card for quality, not a vague impression.
cards.why_it_matters: A concrete, falsifiable metric you can ask a farmer or curing works to report — fits the 'named farm, named price, named quality' brand ethos better than a vague 'good quality' claim.
cards.cloze: ___ grading separates serious defects (Category 1) like black/sour beans from minor ones (Category 2) like broken beans.

cards.definition: A person certified through a rigorous multi-day exam to score green coffee on the standardised SCA 100-point cupping scale.
cards.analogy: Like a wine sommelier's certification, but for coffee cupping.
cards.why_it_matters: A formal cupping score on a Seasonal-tier bag (e.g. 'cupped at 84.5') needs to come from a Q Grader to carry real credibility.
cards.cloze: A ___ is certified to score coffee consistently on the SCA 100-point scale — the coffee world's sommelier equivalent.

cards.definition: Green coffee scoring 80 or above on the 100-point SCA scale is 'specialty grade'; below that, it's commercial/commodity grade regardless of origin story.
cards.analogy: A hard, checkable number, unlike marketing language.
cards.why_it_matters: This is the industry-standard bar your Seasonal-tier coffee needs to clear to legitimately call itself specialty.
cards.cloze: Coffee scoring ___ or above on the SCA 100-point scale is considered specialty grade.

cards.definition: Cup score = the numeric SCA result (how good). Tasting note = descriptive language like 'gooseberry' or 'tamarind' (what it tastes like) — marketing/storytelling, not a formal metric.
cards.analogy: Score answers 'how good'; note answers 'what does it taste like.'
cards.why_it_matters: Both are useful but distinct — your gooseberry and tamarind notes are storytelling language, not a substitute for a formal score.
cards.cloze: A cup score answers 'how good'; a ___ answers 'what does it taste like' (e.g. gooseberry, tamarind).

cards.definition: Seller's price/responsibility ends once goods are loaded onto the ship at the origin port; buyer pays sea freight and insurance from there.
cards.analogy: You hand the bag over at the dock; they take it from there.
cards.why_it_matters: When quoting The Wild to an overseas buyer, FOB (Chennai/Cochin) vs. CIF changes your price by a meaningful margin.
cards.cloze: ___ pricing means the seller's responsibility ends once goods are loaded onto the ship at the origin port.

cards.definition: Seller's quoted price already includes shipping and insurance all the way to the destination port.
cards.analogy: Door-to-door pricing, vs. FOB's dock-only pricing.
cards.why_it_matters: Whether you quote FOB or CIF changes who's liable if something goes wrong in transit.
cards.cloze: ___ pricing means the seller's quote already includes shipping and insurance to the destination port.

cards.definition: An internationally standard numeric classification for every traded good, used by customs everywhere (e.g. HS 090111 = green coffee).
cards.analogy: A universal product barcode category every country's customs reads the same way.
cards.why_it_matters: Tariff rates are set per HS code, not per 'coffee' generally — whether you export green, roasted, or instant genuinely changes your duty rate.
cards.cloze: An ___ is the internationally standard numeric classification customs use for every traded good, including coffee.

cards.definition: The standard default import duty a country charges any WTO member without a special trade deal — the baseline unless an agreement like CETA/CEPA lowers it.
cards.analogy: The rate everyone gets unless they have a special deal.
cards.why_it_matters: UK's MFN rate on roast & ground is 6–8%; under CETA, India specifically gets 0% — a real cost advantage over Brazil or Vietnam.
cards.cloze: The ___ tariff is the standard default import duty a country charges, unless a trade agreement lowers it further.

cards.definition: Bilateral/regional trade agreements lowering tariffs: FTA is the general umbrella term; CEPA is India's deal with Oman/UAE (broader, covers services too); CETA is the India-UK version of the same idea.
cards.analogy: Different names for the same kind of deal, chosen by each partner country.
cards.why_it_matters: These are the mechanisms behind the 0%-duty opportunities already flagged for both Oman and UK.
cards.cloze: India's trade deal with Oman is called ___, while the India-UK version of the same idea is called CETA.

cards.definition: An EU law requiring proof that imported commodities weren't grown on land deforested after a cutoff date, with full supply-chain traceability back to the plot, often GPS-verified.
cards.analogy: The EU saying: 'show us exactly which patch of land this grew on, and prove it was already farmland.'
cards.why_it_matters: Named-farm, named-plot sourcing is exactly the traceability EUDR requires — a genuine tailwind for your model, and why EUDR was flagged as a future demand driver for cacao too.
cards.cloze: The ___ requires GPS-verified, plot-level traceability proving coffee wasn't grown on recently deforested land.

cards.definition: The ICO's single blended reference price for coffee globally, in US cents/lb, weighting different coffee-group prices by their share of world trade.
cards.analogy: Coffee's version of a stock market index like the Nifty 50.
cards.why_it_matters: One number that summarises how the market is doing, even though individual origins move differently underneath it.
cards.cloze: The ___ is the ICO's single blended reference price for coffee globally — like a stock market index for coffee.

cards.definition: The price gap between two coffee categories or lots (e.g. Colombian Milds–Robustas differential). Widening = milder coffees pulling ahead in price; narrowing = gap closing.
cards.analogy: A spread, like the gap between two stock prices.
cards.why_it_matters: Tracking the Arabica-Robusta differential tells you whether The Everyday's Robusta-heavy economics are getting cheaper or pricier relative to Seasonal-tier Arabica sourcing.
cards.cloze: The ___ is the price gap between two coffee categories or lots — useful for tracking Robusta vs. Arabica cost trends.

cards.definition: Coffee graded, warehoused, and officially certified as deliverable against a futures contract on an exchange. Falling stocks signal tightening supply and rising prices; rising stocks signal the opposite.
cards.analogy: The exchange's verified inventory of ready-to-deliver coffee.
cards.why_it_matters: Falling certified stocks is a leading signal that prices may be about to rise.
cards.cloze: ___ are coffee lots graded and warehoused as officially deliverable against a futures contract — falling levels signal tightening supply.

cards.definition: Backwardation: spot price is higher than future prices, signalling urgent scarcity. Contango: future prices are higher than spot, signalling comfortable current supply.
cards.analogy: Backwardation = 'buy now, don't wait.' Contango = 'no rush, supply is comfortable.'
cards.why_it_matters: Backwardation is a signal that locking in supply now, rather than waiting, is the economically rational move.
cards.cloze: In ___, the spot price is higher than future delivery prices, signalling immediate scarcity.

cards.definition: An agreement to buy or sell coffee at a set price for future delivery, locking in the price now regardless of market moves.
cards.analogy: Like pre-booking a hotel room at today's rate for a trip six months out.
cards.why_it_matters: This is the mitigant flagged for green bean price volatility once your volumes justify it — not needed at current small-batch stage.
cards.cloze: A ___ locks in a price now for coffee to be delivered at a future date, regardless of how the market moves.

cards.definition: An informal, non-audited sourcing relationship where the buyer deals directly with the farmer or farmer group, cutting out middlemen, paying a negotiated price rather than a certification-mandated floor.
cards.analogy: This is essentially your model — and Black Baza's.
cards.why_it_matters: It's the foundation your brand's named-farm, named-price claims are built on.
cards.cloze: ___ is an informal sourcing relationship where the buyer deals directly with the farmer, cutting out middlemen.

cards.definition: Third-party certifications: Fair Trade guarantees a minimum floor price plus social premium; Rainforest Alliance audits environmental/social farming standards; NPOP certifies absence of synthetic inputs.
cards.analogy: Each is a different kind of audited logo, answering a different question.
cards.why_it_matters: Your brand favours named, falsifiable direct claims over these logos — but they matter more in export/EUDR contexts.
cards.cloze: ___ certification guarantees a minimum floor price plus a social premium paid to the farmer group.

cards.definition: Increasing specificity of provenance claims: single-origin (one country/region, could be hundreds of farms), micro-lot (small segregated batch from one farm/section), named-lot (specific farm, plot, elevation, farmer's name — the strongest claim).
cards.analogy: Three zoom levels on the same map — country, farm, plot.
cards.why_it_matters: 'Named-lot' is the strongest, most falsifiable claim — the one your Araku-inspired model is built around.
cards.cloze: A ___ is a micro-lot with the specific farm, plot, elevation and farmer's name attached — the strongest provenance claim.

cards.definition: Paying an existing roaster a per-kg fee to roast your own green beans to your own spec, without owning roasting equipment yourself.
cards.analogy: Like using a shared commercial kitchen to bake your own recipe, rather than buying an oven.
cards.why_it_matters: This is exactly the arrangement with Adrian at GB Roasters and the Blue Tokai Seetharampalya backup.
cards.cloze: ___ means paying an existing roaster a per-kg fee to roast your own beans without owning the equipment.

cards.definition: Private label: you supply a product carrying someone else's brand name, no visible trace of you. Branded: the product carries your own brand name and story, even when sold B2B.
cards.analogy: Invisible supplier vs. visible name on the bag.
cards.why_it_matters: The Bhive/Bowring Club pitch is deliberately branded, not private label — 'IP stays with you' — which is why it builds brand equity even as a B2B sale.
cards.cloze: In a ___ deal, the product carries your own brand name and story even when sold B2B, unlike private label.

cards.definition: Shorthand for Hotels, Restaurants, and Cafés — the standard industry term for the B2B foodservice channel, distinct from retail.
cards.analogy: The channel label for The Everyday's target buyers.
cards.why_it_matters: This is the standard term for exactly the channel Bowring Club and Century Club sit in.
cards.cloze: ___ is the industry shorthand for Hotels, Restaurants, and Cafés — the B2B foodservice channel.

cards.definition: The true total cost of a product once it reaches you — bean cost + transport + toll roasting fee + packaging + duties — vs. just the farm-gate price.
cards.analogy: The real number, not the headline price you paid the farmer.
cards.why_it_matters: The ₹700 cost-to-produce-a-kg figure in the Action Plan is essentially a landed cost calculation.
cards.cloze: ___ is the true total cost once a product reaches you — beans, transport, roasting, packaging and duties combined.

cards.definition: The time gap between paying cash out (beans, roaster fees) and receiving cash in (client payment). Longer cycle = more cash reserves needed idle.
cards.analogy: How long your money is 'out' before it comes back to be reused.
cards.why_it_matters: This is exactly what the '15-day invoice terms, 2 months of bean stock' risk mitigant is managing.
cards.cloze: The ___ is the gap between paying cash out for beans/roasting and receiving cash in from client invoices.

cards.definition: GST: tax registration for turnover thresholds or cross-state/B2B sales. FSSAI: food safety license required for any food product, including coffee. Udyam/MSME: registers you as a small enterprise for scheme eligibility and institutional-buyer preference.
cards.analogy: Three separate, commonly-confused registrations — tax, safety, and enterprise status.
cards.why_it_matters: Bowring Club and Century Club are institutional buyers — worth checking early if they require an FSSAI number or GST invoice, since these have lead time.
cards.cloze: ___ is the food safety license required for anyone manufacturing or selling a food product, including coffee.
